channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Cutesy Raising Sim Magical Princess Hits 100k Sales in Two Days
item.content:encoded:
It's only been two days since anime raising sim Magical Princess was released on Steam, but the game has already managed to pick up an impressive number of sales for itself, developers MAGI and Neotro have revealed.

Magical Princess follows on from the likes of Long Live the Queen and Volcano Princess, although it's arguably a more wholesome affair than at least the former. Clearly, that's struck a chord with players, as it's racked up 100,000 sales in its first two days.

MAGI and Neotro say that they "never imagined that so many people would be playing [their] game", and that all of the messages they're receiving from players "continue to inspire and motivate [their] team". They're also actively working to fix bugs identified by players.

Clearly, those bugs haven't bothered the player base of Magical Princess too much, because the game is currently sitting at an Overwhelmingly Positive Steam rating across almost two thousand reviews, and that's not an easy rating to get.

If you're wondering what Magical Princess is, it's a raising sim in which you'll guide your daughter Alice "from early childhood all the way to graduation at the magical academy". Raising sims are effectively life sims in which you guide the decisions and stats of another character, usually a child or ward.

There are plenty of options available for Alice; you can, of course, guide her down the path of a straight-A, academically-gifted student, but you can also turn her into a delinquent biker or even "the demon queen". How she grows up is entirely up to you.

MAGI and Neotro promise "over 170 date scenes", "over 50 endings", and a cast of more than 30 characters for you and your daughter to meet over the course of Magical Princess.

Magical Princess is available right now for PC via Steam. Stay tuned for more.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph

item.title: Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era Tops 250k Sales in One Day
item.content:encoded:
Developer Unfrozen, alongside publisher Hooded Horse and IP owner Ubisoft, have announced that their turn-based strategy revival Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era has crossed a big sales milestone in its first day.

First announced back in 2024, Olden Era was released yesterday, and it looks like it's already struck a chord with fans looking for a return to the old-school values of the Might and Magic series, because it's racked up 250,000 sales in that time.

In a press release, the aforementioned three studios confirm that it actually only took Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era 16 of its first 24 hours to achieve the sales figure, so presumably, it's all smooth sailing from here on out for the game.

As the first new Heroes of Might and Magic game in more than a decade, this is an auspicious start for Olden Era, but it's got a long way to go before its 1.0 release. Unfrozen and Hooded Horse say plenty is planned for Early Access, including more campaign maps, pre-made scenarios, and a Spectator Mode.

Hooded Horse's Tim Bender says it's an "absolute thrill" to see Olden Era sell so well, praising the franchise's "incredible history". 

What's more, the game was probably developed without the use of generative AI, at least if Hooded Horse's contracts are anything to go by (although Ubisoft's fondness for the tech might supersede that particular requirement).

If you're wondering exactly what Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era is, it's the newest installment in the Heroes of Might and Magic series, which began all the way back in 1995. Subsequent titles were sporadically released between then and 2015, at which point the series took a ten-year break.

You can check out Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era right now on PC via Steam Early Access, and it's also available on PC Game Pass (until the next seismic tier change, that is).

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph
